finding.contract_claim: document.getElementById("root") returns HTMLElement | null, so the non-null assertion `document.getElementById("root")!` passed to ReactDOM.createRoot will raise at runtime if the element is missing.
finding.observed_behavior: The code asserts non-null with `!` instead of a runtime check; the accompanying comment states the element is known to exist in index.html (standard React 18 bootstrapping pattern).
finding.triage_reasoning: This is the canonical React 18 bootstrapping pattern, and the code explicitly documents the invariant in a comment: "we know the element exists in index.html." The `!` non-null assertion is the standard TypeScript idiom for this well-understood pattern. The 'gap' is intentional and deliberately documented; there is no mismatch between what the code claims and what it does. Significance fails: converting this to a runtime guard would be unusual and adds no value when the HTML template is known. This is an ecosystem convention.
